perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall indemnify and hold harmless, Fruit of Life Productions LLC, its related entities, partners, agents, officers, directors, employees, attorneys, heirs, successors, and assigns from against any and all claims, losses, damages, judgments, settlements, costs and expenses (including reasonable attorney's fees and expenses), and liabilities of every kind incurred as a result of: (i) any act or omission by Sponsor or its officers, directors, entities, employees, agents; (ii) any use of Sponsor's name, logo, Website, or other information, products, or service provided by Sponsor; and/or (iii) the inaccuracy or breach of any of the covenants, representations and warranties made by Sponsor in this Agreement. (iv) any changes in company value or brand value.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless, Fruit of Life Productions LLC, its related entities, partners, agents, officers, directors, employees, attorneys, heirs, successors, and assigns from against claims, losses, damages, judgments, settlements, costs and expenses, and liabilities incurred as a result of: (i) any act or omission by Sponsor or its officers, directors, entities, employees, agents; (ii) any use of Sponsor's name, logo, Website, or other information, products, or service provided by Sponsor; and/or (iii) the inaccuracy or breach of any of the covenants, representations and warranties made by Sponsor in this Agreement. (iv) changes in company value or brand value.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall indemnify and hold harmless' to 'shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless' weakens the obligation. The removal of 'any and all' and 'of every kind' also reduces the scope of indemnification. Under Florida law, indemnification clauses must be clear and unambiguous to be enforceable. 'Endeavor to indemnify' introduces ambiguity as it does not guarantee actual indemnification, potentially conflicting with the expectation of complete protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 725.06 concerns agreements to indemnify or hold harmless. While it does not explicitly prohibit ambiguous language, the courts interpret indemnification agreements strictly, requiring clarity and unambiguity. By changing 'shall indemnify' to 'shall endeavor to indemnify', the agreement becomes ambiguous and less enforceable, potentially undermining the protection Fruit of Life Productions LLC expects. Florida courts require specific and unequivocal language in indemnification agreements.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XLI > Chapter 725 > Section 06 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 725 UNENFORCEABLE CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 06 Construction contracts; limitation on indemnification. F.S. 725.06 725.06 Construction contracts; limitation on indemnification. — (1) Any portion of any agreement or contract for or in connection with, or any guarantee of or in connection with, any construction, alteration, repair, or demolition of a building, structure, appurtenance, or appliance, including moving and excavating associated therewith, between an owner of real property and an architect, engineer, general contractor, subcontractor, sub-subcontractor, or materialman or any combination thereof wherein any party referred to herein promises to indemnify or hold harmless the other party to the agreement, contract, or guarantee for liability for damages to persons or property caused in whole or in part by any act, omission, or default of the indemnitee arising from the contract or its performance, shall be void and unenforceable unless the contract contains a monetary limitation on the extent of the indemnification that bears a reasonable commercial relationship to the contract and is part of the project specifications or bid documents, if any. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the monetary limitation on the extent of the indemnification provided to the owner of real property by any party in privity of contract with such owner shall not be less than $1 million per occurrence, unless otherwise agreed by the parties. Indemnification provisions in any such agreements, contracts, or guarantees may not require that the indemnitor indemnify the indemnitee for damages to persons or property caused in whole or in part by any act, omission, or default of a party other than: (a) The indemnitor; (b) Any of the indemnitor’s contractors, subcontractors, sub-subcontractors, materialmen, or agents of any tier or their respective employees; or (c) The indemnitee or its officers, directors, agents, or employees. However, such indemnification shall not include claims of, or damages resulting from, gross negligence, or willful, wanton or intentional misconduct of the indemnitee or its officer
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall indemnify and hold harmless' to 'shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless' introduces ambiguity regarding the Sponsor's obligation. The original text clearly mandates indemnification, whereas the modified text suggests an attempt to indemnify, which may not guarantee actual compensation. The removal of 'any and all' and 'of every kind' further weakens the scope of indemnification. Florida Statute § 725.06, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, concerns construction contracts and indemnification. While the snippet focuses on monetary limitations and specific scenarios within construction, the underlying principle is that indemnification agreements must be clear and unambiguous. The altered text introduces ambiguity, undermining the expected protection and potentially rendering the agreement less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, each party shall use and reproduce the other party's Confidential Information only for purposes of this Agreement with written authorization by disclosing party, and only to the extent necessary for such purpose. Each party shall restrict disclosure of the other party's Confidential Information to its employees and agents with a reasonable need to know such Confidential Information, and shall not disclose the other party's Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, each party should consider using and reproducing the other party's Confidential Information for purposes of this Agreement with written authorization by disclosing party, and to the extent they find necessary for such purpose. Each party should consider restricting disclosure of the other party's Confidential Information to its employees and agents and should avoid disclosing the other party's Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall' with 'should consider' and 'shall not' with 'should avoid' significantly weakens the mandatory nature of protecting confidential information. This introduces ambiguity about the level of commitment to confidentiality. While Florida doesn't have a single statute mandating specific confidentiality measures in all contracts, trade secret protection under the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. This modification could be interpreted as not constituting reasonable efforts, especially if sensitive information is leaked.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA), Florida Statutes § 688.002
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 688.002 defines misappropriation of trade secrets, but the key to trade secret protection under FUTSA is taking 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Changing the language from 'shall' to 'should consider' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. If a party fails to adequately protect confidential information due to this ambiguous language, it could be argued that they did not take reasonable efforts as required by FUTSA, thus jeopardizing trade secret protection.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses 'shall' and 'shall not,' creating a mandatory obligation to protect confidential information. The changed language uses 'should consider' and 'should avoid,' weakening this obligation and introducing ambiguity. While there are no law snippets, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that Florida law (FUTSA) requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. The modified language weakens these obligations and therefore could be interpreted as not constituting reasonable efforts, which would be a contradiction of trade secret protection requirements.
